Now, next tonality is absolutely crucial for the first few seconds of the sale. It's crucial elsewhere also, but in those first few seconds, there's this tone that you have when you first get on the phone, they first hear your voice that establishes a couple of different things. Now, watch the difference here. When I walk into a room of telemarketers or salespeople, typically they're making their calls, they'll pick up the phone and they'll say, yes, hi, Mr. Jones, how are you today? At that level or Mr. Jones, hi, yes, how are you today? Yes, good, thanks for asking. That's this sort of level of enthusiasm versus the person that's, hi, Mr. Jones there. Hey, Mr. Jones, it's Jordan Robert. How are you doing today? How are you going today? And that happy, like how are you going today? How are you doing today? That sort of upbeat, enthusiastic, happy tone. Now you're probably saying, oh, Jordan, that that is so stupid. Come on. You need to tell me that if I just, you know, sound happy and peppy and bursting with love, people are going to actually listen to me. Guess what? The answer is yes. People cannot help because we have some powerful force inside us. It's called reciprocity, which I'm going to get to later. It's a fundamental law of influence here that when someone gives us something first, whether it's even a compliment or a pleasant greeting, we almost have this unconscious obligation to return that in kind. So if I pick up the phone and I say, Mr. Jones, how are you doing today? Versus, yeah, Mr. Jones, how are you doing today? One says, yeah, Mr. Jones, how are you doing today? I've been doing this crap for so long and I don't really care how you're doing. I'm just asking you to be kind and nice because I want to get and rip your throat out and sell you something. That's what the basic tone says versus, yeah, Mr. Jones, how are you doing today? Meaning, I really want to know because I actually care about you. If we go back for a second now and we say one of the key elements of rapport is what? I care. So by this simple act of speaking to someone like you actually give a damn about them, you dramatically increase the response rate. So now all of a sudden watch, you say, wow, wait, actually, this is actually better than I thought. So not only can I phrase something as a question, then I followed up with a really pleasant, not like, John, how are you doing today? Oh my God. I'm like, this guy's crazy. You know, hang up while you think you're out of your mind, right? But that sort of sweet spot and I'm going to give it to you a few times so you can mirror this is a sweet spot. It's like, John, how are you going today? How are you doing today? Not, John, how are you doing today? You're not like Tony the Tiger. It's great, right? So if John says to you, Tony, watch the difference. So he says to you, yeah, I'm doing good. You don't say great. Oh, thank you. You sound like an idiot, right? But he says to you, he says, oh, I'm pretty good. You don't say great. You know how many times I've been in rooms full of salespeople where the customer, they'll ask that question, you know, how you do in the client will say, I'm okay. And the salesman will say, oh, hey, well, that's good. You don't think that the sales, the client on the other end says, alert, alert, alert. This person doesn't care about me. He's just trying to sell me something. Let me tell you something. It's so profoundly important that your tonality is in those first few seconds that that person just says, wow, this is not like the other people I speak to. This person actually cares. This person actually cares how I feel. Now watch what would happen without tonality. See tonality, it's very cold to the secret weapon of influence. Here's why it's the secret weapon because it's an unspoken language of communication. You're actually saying things that they're hearing without you saying it and without them knowing it. So I say to you, watch, you're John, how are you going today? And you're like, wow, he really cares. I couldn't say to you, John, how are you going today? I really want to know because I really care about you. He'd say, what an idiot this person is. How disingenuous is that? They would say, what's stupid? Oh, he just told me he really cares. He doesn't even know me. You could never say the words. The words simply don't exist in a greeting to say to someone, I really care about you. I really want to know. There's no words for that, but there's a tonality for it. So each of these tonalities, they essentially chunk up to this very, very high level of rapport which says, I care. I really want to know. I have your best interest at heart. All those things that open up a person to be influenced. And as salespeople, that's what we do. We influence people to go from a state where they're kind of certain. They think it's pretty good. We influence them to a state where they're absolutely certain about our product. That's what we do. And tonality, that ability to manage your voice in a way where you create an unconscious dialogue in the other person. They actually start hearing words that you're not saying. You end up controlling the conversation every step of the way and have their unconscious dialogue working for you versus against you.
